summary: DeVault argues that future generations will judge our era harshly, much as we judge the Middle Ages. He contends that humanity has solved the problem of natural scarcity yet deliberately chooses to deny people basic needs in pursuit of profit. He connects the Capitol insurrection to the tech industry's role in building tools for radicalization and propaganda, implicating himself and his peers as complicit. The post broadens to a global critique of declining democracy, corporate political capture, and capitalism's corrosive effects on society, concluding that climate destruction may ensure no one survives to remember us at all.
key_points: Humanity has eliminated natural scarcity for basic resources but chooses to let people suffer and die for profit
The tech industry built tools that became the most powerful instruments for censorship, propaganda, and radicalization in history
Corporate wealth rivals nation-states and has captured political systems, rendering average citizens' opinions statistically negligible
Democracy is declining globally, with authoritarian and populist movements advancing across multiple continents
The relentless pursuit of profit is rendering the Earth uninhabitable, potentially ensuring no future generation survives to judge us
money_quotes: We have achieved what one may consider the single unifying goal of the entire history of humanity: we have eliminated natural scarcity for our basic resources.
We have more empty homes than we have homeless people.
What we hadn't realized is that we were also building the most powerful tool the world has ever seen for censorship, propaganda, and radicalization.
As a computer engineer, I am complicit in this radicalization.
We are at one end of a process which launders money to wash off the blood. Morals are dead.
Our entire society boils down to one measure: profit. We are being eaten alive by capitalism.
Perhaps future generations won't remember us after all, considering that in that same relentless pursuit of profits we are vigorously rendering the Earth uninhabitable.
key_insight: The tech industry's utopian promise of global connectivity instead produced history's most powerful machinery for radicalization and corporate control, making technologists complicit in the broader societal regression driven by unchecked capitalism.
